title: Day 01 Interview Prep
description: Practice explaining React fundamentals the way frontend interviews at product companies expect — clear, concise, with trade-offs.

questions.question: Why React?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Do you understand the problem React solves, or are you reciting marketing copy?
keyPoints: Declarative UI — describe what UI should look like for a given state
Efficient updates via reconciliation / Virtual DOM
questions.sampleAnswer: React helps teams build complex interactive UIs by letting developers compose reusable components and declare UI as a function of state. Instead of manually updating the DOM, React computes minimal changes. I would choose React for large SPAs with rich interactivity; for mostly static content I might pick simpler tools.
followUps: When would you NOT choose React?
How does React compare to Vue or Svelte for your use case?
redFlags: React is the best framework for everything

questions.question: What is JSX and how is it different from HTML?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Basic syntax knowledge and awareness that JSX is compiled.
keyPoints: JSX is syntax extension compiled to React.createElement
Stricter than HTML — must return single parent or fragment
questions.sampleAnswer: JSX lets me write UI that looks like HTML inside JavaScript. It compiles to React.createElement calls. It differs from HTML: I use className instead of class, embed expressions with {}, and must close all tags. It is not sent to the browser directly — the build step compiles it.
followUps: What happens to JSX during build?
Can you use if statements directly in JSX?

questions.question: What is a functional component?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Can you define the building block of modern React apps?
keyPoints: Preferred over class components in modern codebases
questions.sampleAnswer: A functional component is a JavaScript function — usually arrow or function declaration — that accepts props and returns JSX. It is the standard way to build UI in modern React. I keep components small and typed with TypeScript props interfaces.
followUps: How would you type props?
When were class components used?

questions.question: Why Vite instead of older React tooling?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Awareness of developer experience and modern bundling.
keyPoints: Native ES modules in dev for fast cold start
questions.sampleAnswer: Vite uses native ES modules during development so the dev server starts almost instantly and HMR is fast. For production it bundles with Rollup. Compared to older webpack-based setups, the feedback loop is significantly better, which matters for team productivity.
followUps: What is HMR?
What runs in production — the dev server or a build?

questions.question: Explain let vs const and why it matters in React code.
questions.whatInterviewerTests: JS fundamentals and immutability awareness before state topics.
keyPoints: const prevents rebinding, not deep immutability
Avoid var due to hoisting/function scope
Immutability habits prepare for state updates
questions.sampleAnswer: I default to const because it prevents accidental reassignment and documents intent. let is for values that truly change, like loop indices. var is avoided in modern code. In React, state updates should produce new references rather than mutating objects — const helps build that mental habit.
followUps: Does const make arrays immutable?
What happens if you mutate state directly?

questions.question: Why use TypeScript with React?
keyPoints: Prop type safety at component boundaries
Trade-off: setup cost vs long-term maintainability
questions.sampleAnswer: TypeScript catches prop and API shape errors before runtime and makes refactors safer in large codebases. I type component props explicitly and let inference handle locals. The trade-off is slightly more upfront work, but it pays off as the team and codebase grow.
followUps: What is type inference?
How do you type children props?

questions.question: Explain the Virtual DOM at a high level. What problem does it solve?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Conceptual depth without over-claiming performance expertise.
keyPoints: Not always faster than manual DOM — trade-off is developer productivity
questions.sampleAnswer: React maintains a lightweight tree representing the UI. On state change it builds a new tree, diffs against the previous, and updates only what changed in the real DOM. The goal is predictable, efficient updates without manual DOM manipulation. I would not claim Virtual DOM is always faster — for simple pages direct DOM can be fine — but for complex UIs the model scales better for teams.
followUps: What is reconciliation?
When might React be slower than vanilla JS?

questions.question: How would you structure a small React project on day one so it scales to a larger app?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Folder structure thinking and separation of concerns early.
keyPoints: Flat components folder OK initially; feature folders later
questions.sampleAnswer: I start with src/components for UI pieces, each in its own file with typed props. App.tsx only composes layout. I keep data arrays outside JSX and map to components. As the app grows, I group by feature — features/auth, features/dashboard — and extract shared UI to components/. I never put fetch logic in presentational components even later.
followUps: When do you split into feature folders?
Where do types live?

questions.question: You join a team and see a 400-line App.tsx with Navbar, Hero, and API calls mixed together. What do you recommend?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Refactoring judgment without rewriting everything day one.
keyPoints: Move data fetching to hooks or services later
Incremental refactor — ship value between steps
questions.sampleAnswer: I would not rewrite in one PR. First I extract Navbar, Hero, and Footer into components with typed props — behavior unchanged, tests or manual QA pass. Next I move static data to constants. If API calls exist, I extract a custom hook in a follow-up PR. I document the plan so the team sees incremental improvement, not a big-bang refactor.
followUps: How do you prevent regressions during refactor?
What metrics convince a manager to allow refactor time?

questions.question: A junior developer says React is slow because Virtual DOM is heavy. How do you respond in a code review discussion?
keyPoints: Virtual DOM overhead exists but is usually not the first bottleneck
Real issues: unnecessary re-renders, large bundles, layout thrashing
questions.sampleAnswer: I would agree that abstraction has cost, but challenge the assumption without profiling. React is slow usually because of unnecessary re-renders, huge component trees, or unoptimized assets — not Virtual DOM itself. I would suggest React DevTools Profiler and bundle analysis before architectural changes. If the UI is truly simple and static, simpler tools might be appropriate — context matters.
followUps: What tools would you use to profile?
Name one optimization for later weeks
